elements.sent-2411-005: Chowdhury et al. \institute{ECE, Purdue University, West Lafayette, IN 47907, USA \and Apple Inc., Cupertino, CA 95014, USA \and Neutron Scattering Division, ORNL, Oak Ridge, TN 37830, USA \and Electrical \& Engineering Infrastructure Div., ORNL, Oak Ridge, TN 37831, USA \and NASA Goddard Space Flight Center, Greenbelt, MD 20771, USA \and Department of Mathematics, Purdue University, West Lafayette, IN 47907, USA $^a$, $^b$, $^c$, $^d$, $^e$, $^f$, $^g$, $^h$ \let\thefootnote\relax\footnote{ This manuscript has been authored by UT-Battelle, LLC, under contract DE-AC05-00OR22725 with the US Department of Energy (DOE).}}
elements.sent-2411-006: The US government retains and the publisher, by accepting the article for publication, acknowledges that the US government retains a nonexclusive, paid-up, irrevocable, worldwide license to publish or reproduce the published form of this manuscript, or allow others to do so, for US government purposes.
elements.sent-2411-007: DOE will provide public access to these results of federally sponsored research in accordance with the DOE Public Access Plan (http://energy.gov/downloads/doe-public-access-plan).
elements.sent-2411-008: Hyperspectral neutron computed tomography enables 3D non-destructive imaging of the spectral characteristics of materials.
elements.sent-2411-009: In traditional hyperspectral reconstruction, the data for each neutron wavelength bin is reconstructed separately.
elements.sent-2411-010: This per-bin reconstruction is extremely time-consuming due to the typically large number of wavelength bins.
elements.sent-2411-011: Furthermore, these reconstructions may suffer from severe artifacts due to the low signal-to-noise ratio in each wavelength bin.
elements.sent-2411-012: We present a novel fast hyperspectral reconstruction algorithm for computationally efficient and accurate reconstruction of hyperspectral neutron data.
elements.sent-2411-013: Our algorithm uses a subspace extraction procedure that transforms hyperspectral data into low-dimensional data within an intermediate subspace.
elements.sent-2411-014: This step effectively reduces data dimensionality and spectral noise.
elements.sent-2411-015: High-quality reconstructions are then performed within this low-dimensional subspace.
elements.sent-2411-016: Finally, the algorithm expands the subspace reconstructions into hyperspectral reconstructions.
elements.sent-2411-017: We apply our algorithm to measured neutron data and demonstrate that it reduces computation and improves reconstruction quality compared to the conventional approach.
elements.sent-2411-018: Neutron computed tomography (nCT) enables volumetric reconstruction of a sample from radiographs recorded by exposing the sample to a neutron source at multiple angles.
elements.sent-2411-019: Importantly, nCT provides information that is complementary to traditional X-ray CT because neutrons interact directly with the nucleus rather than the electron cloud \cite{vlassenbroeck2007nctvsxct .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Hyperspectral neutron computed tomography (HSnCT) is a more advanced technique, in which a pulsed neutron source is used to illuminate a sample and a time-of-flight detector is used to measure the projection images across a range of wavelengths - potentially of the order of a few thousand.
elements.sent-2411-021: Using HSnCT, it is possible to analyze material characteristics like crystallographic phases \cite{woracek20143d and isotopic compositions \cite{balke2021epithermal .}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figs/overview.png}} \caption{Overview of the fast hyperspectral reconstruction (FHR) algorithm. FHR reduces the spectral dimension during subspace extraction and restores it during subspace expansion. In between, FHR performs tomographic reconstruction to transition from the sinogram domain to the spatial domain.} \label{fig:overview}
elements.sent-2411-022: HSnCT reconstruction is challenging since the data from each of the possibly thousands of neutron wavelength bins must be reconstructed into individual 3D volumes.
elements.sent-2411-023: Direct hyperspectral reconstruction (DHR) \cite{daugherty2023hyperrecon is the most established solution to this problem.}
elements.sent-2411-024: In DHR, the data from each wavelength bin is reconstructed separately using the filtered back projection (FBP) algorithm since it is computationally fast.
elements.sent-2411-025: However, DHR still tends to be very computationally expensive since it may require thousands of 3D tomographic reconstructions.
elements.sent-2411-026: In addition, FBP typically requires a large number of projections (also referred to as views) with high signal-to-noise ratios (SNR) to avoid severe reconstruction artifacts.
elements.sent-2411-027: This presents another challenge since the measurement of individual hyperspectral neutron radiographs with a high SNR is typically very slow, making it impractical in the context of HSnCT.
elements.sent-2411-028: Consequently, DHR reconstructions often suffer from significant noise and artifacts due to the low SNR of each spectral component.
elements.sent-2411-029: In this paper, we present a fast hyperspectral reconstruction (FHR) algorithm for effective and efficient reconstruction of HSnCT data.
elements.sent-2411-030: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:overview , the first step of FHR is a subspace extraction procedure \cite{chowdhury2023amd that transforms the high-dimensional hyperspectral measurements into low-dimensional subspace projection views.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: FHR then performs reconstruction within this low-dimensional subspace.
elements.sent-2411-032: Finally, FHR expands the subspace reconstructions into hyperspectral reconstructions.
elements.sent-2411-033: The use of subspace extraction in our algorithm serves two purposes:.
elements.sent-2411-034: Improves SNR in the reconstruction by up to 16dB by approximating the data in a low-dimensional subspace.
elements.sent-2411-035: Reduces computation time by up to a factor of 10 due to the reduced number of tomographic reconstructions.
elements.sent-2411-036: We apply our algorithm to measured neutron data collected at the Oak Ridge National Laboratory Spallation Neutron Source SNAP beamline and demonstrate that it is substantially faster and yields better results than the traditional method.
elements.figure-2411-002: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figs/imaging_system.png}} \caption{Illustration of a hyperspectral imaging setup at a spallation neutron source. The arrows on the left represent a pulsed neutron source, which generates a beam of neutrons across a range of wavelengths. The beam travels through the sample to a $N_r \times N_c$ time-of-flight (TOF) detector, which produces wavelength-resolved radiographs for $N_k=1200$ bins. The right plot shows neutron counts across the wavelength bins for a single detector pixel. Such data are collected for $N_v$ orientations of the sample.} \label{fig:img_sys}
elements.sent-2411-037: Figure~\ref{fig:img_sys illustrates a standard hyperspectral neutron imaging system that is used to collect wavelength-resolved hyperspectral data from multiple orientations of the sample at a pulsed neutron source \cite{ nelson2018sns .}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The pulse of neutrons passes through the sample and is detected by a 2D time-of-flight (ToF) imaging array \cite{tremsin2012tof .}
elements.sent-2411-039: The ToF detector counts the number of neutrons at each pixel and for each time interval bin.
elements.sent-2411-040: These time interval bins then correspond to each neutron's velocity or wavelength.
elements.sent-2411-041: The specific relationship between the neutron time of flight, $\Delta t$, and the wavelength, $\lambda$, is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:wave_tof} \lambda = \frac{h}{m_n} \frac{\Delta t}{L} \ ,\end{equation} where $h$ is Planck's constant, $m_n$ is the neutron mass, and $L$ is the distance between the source and the detector.
elements.sent-2411-042: We now introduce the following notation to describe the relevant quantities:.
elements.sent-2411-043: The output of the ToF detector is a hyperspectral neutron radiograph in the form of a $N_r \times N_c \times N_k$ array.
elements.sent-2411-044: In our experiment, we used $N_r=N_c=512$ and $N_k=1200$.
elements.sent-2411-045: So, a single hyperspectral radiograph in our experiment contains approximately $300$ megapixels.
elements.sent-2411-046: In order to perform a tomographic scan, the object is rotated to $N_v$ orientations \cite{yang2023orientation, tang2024orientation and at each orientation, a hyperspectral radiograph, $y_{v,r,c,k}$, is measured where $v,r,c,k$ represent the discrete view, row, column, and wavelength indices.}
elements.sent-2411-047: In addition, a single hyperspectral radiograph is measured with the object removed, which we denote by $y^o_{r,c,k}$.
elements.sent-2411-048: From this, we can compute the normalized hyperspectral projection views $p$ as \begin{equation}\label{eq:neglogattenuation} p_{v,r,c,k} = -\log \left( \frac {y_{v,r,c,k}} {y_{r,c,k}^o} \right) \ .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-049: We note that in practice, various corrections must be made in the calculation of \eqref{eq:neglogattenuation to account for effects like scatter, detector bias, and detector drift \cite{balke2021epithermal .}}
elements.figure-2411-003: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figs/FHR_pipeline.png}} \caption{Illustration of the FHR algorithm. FHR first transforms the $N_k=1200$ dimensional hyperspectral views into $N_s=9$ dimensional subspace views. Then, it performs tomographic reconstruction to produce 9 subspace reconstructions. Finally, it expands the 9 subspace reconstructions into 1200 hyperspectral reconstructions using the basis vectors of the subspace.} \label{fig:FHR_pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-050: Figure~\ref{fig:FHR_pipeline illustrates the three steps in FHR consisting of subspace extraction, tomographic reconstruction, and subspace expansion.}
elements.sent-2411-051: FHR reduces the spectral dimension during subspace extraction, then performs volumetric reconstruction in this lower dimensional space, and finally restores the spectral dimension using subspace expansion.
elements.sent-2411-052: In more colorful terms, the algorithm is ``dehydrating'' the data in the sinogram domain and then ``rehydrating'' in the reconstruction domain.
elements.sent-2411-053: We use the following notation in the remaining sections:.
elements.sent-2411-054: The subspace dimension $N_s$ is chosen such that $N_s<<N_k$.
elements.sent-2411-055: The decomposition can be obtained by solving the non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) \cite{pauca2006nmf problem.}
elements.sent-2411-056: Since $N_s<<N_k$, operations in the subspace domain are much faster than in the hyperspectral domain.
elements.sent-2411-057: Also, empirically, the residual difference from the decomposition $\epsilon = p-V^s (D^s)^\top$ is primarily spectral noise.
elements.sent-2411-058: This greatly reduces the noise in the final reconstructions.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.95\linewidth]{figs/subspace_extraction.png} \caption{Subspace extraction: FHR employs NMF to decompose the $N_k=1200$ dimensional hyperspectral views ($p$) into $N_s=9$ dimensional subspace views ($V^s$) and corresponding subspace basis vectors ($D^s$). This approach effectively reduces data dimensions and also eliminates significant spectral noise.} \label{fig:sub_ext}
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=7cm]{figs/reconstruction.png} \caption{Tomographic reconstruction: FHR computes $N_s=9$ reconstructions from the extracted 9 sets of subspace views using MBIR.} \label{fig:recon}
elements.sent-2411-059: MBIR is well known to produce superior reconstruction when dealing with sparse and low SNR measurements - hence is the ideal choice for HSnCT \cite{venkat2021hyperspectral .}
elements.sent-2411-060: In order to perform MBIR, FHR uses the recently released svMBIR Python package \cite{svmbir-code .}
elements.sent-2411-061: We note that MBIR reconstruction tends to be much slower than FBP reconstruction, but since MBIR enables sparse view reconstruction, it can effectively reduce the data acquisition time for HSnCT.
elements.sent-2411-062: Also, the increased reconstruction time for MBIR is of much less concern since FHR only requires the reconstruction of $N_s$ volumes rather than the $N_k$ 3D volumes required for DHR.
elements.sent-2411-063: Since $D^s$ maps each voxel from subspace to hyperspectral coordinates, the resulting hyperspectral reconstruction is of size $x^h \in \mathbb{R ^{N_x \times N_k}$.}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figs/subspace_expansion.PNG} \caption{Subspace expansion: FHR expands the $N_s=9$ subspace reconstructions ($x^s$) to $N_k=1200$ hyperspectral reconstructions ($x^h$) using the subspace basis vectors ($D^s$).} \label{fig:sub_expansion}
elements.figure-2411-007: \begin{minipage}[a]{0.5\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figs/hyper_recons_real_DHR.png} \centerline{(a) Baseline Method (DHR)} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}[a]{0.5\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figs/hyper_recons_real_FHR.png} \centerline{(b) FHR} \end{minipage} \caption{Hyperspectral reconstruction with measured data for a moon-rock sample: (a) using the baseline method (DHR) and (b) using FHR. The images shown are three different slices from the 3D volume at three different wavelength bins. FHR reconstructions have significantly less noise and artifacts compared to the baseline method.} \label{fig:recon_hyper_real}
elements.figure-2411-008: \begin{center} \caption{Quantitative comparison of our method (FHR) to the baseline method.} \label{table:hr_real} \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|} Algorithm SNR Computation Time Baseline Method (DHR) -3.09 dB 557.03 min FHR 12.97 dB 50.87 min \end{tabular} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-064: Below we present HSnCT results for a Moon rock sample from Apollo Mission 14 measured at the Spallation Neutron Source at Oak Ridge National Laboratory.
elements.sent-2411-065: In this case, HSnCT data was collected with $N_v=53$ views, $N_r=N_c=512$, and $N_k=1200$ hyperspectral wavelength bins.
elements.sent-2411-066: This results in 3D volumes of size $512\times 512\times 512$.
elements.sent-2411-067: We used $N_s=9$ for the dimension of the subspace.
elements.sent-2411-068: Figure~\ref{fig:recon_hyper_real presents hyperspectral reconstructions for the measured data.}
elements.sent-2411-069: Notice that the FHR reconstructions have much less noise than the DHR reconstructions.
elements.sent-2411-070: This is because the initial subspace extraction removes the noise that is orthogonal to the $N_s=9$ dimensional space.
elements.sent-2411-071: Table~\ref{table:hr_real shows a quantitative performance comparison between DHR and FHR.}
elements.sent-2411-072: For this dataset, FHR resulted in an SNR that is approximately 16dB higher than DHR.
elements.sent-2411-073: Moreover, FHR was over 10 times faster than DHR, highlighting the computational efficiency of our approach.
elements.sent-2411-074: We present a fast hyperspectral reconstruction (FHR) algorithm for processing HSnCT data that produces higher-quality reconstructions with dramatically less computation when compared to a conventional direct HSnCT reconstruction method.
elements.sent-2411-075: The intermediate subspace extraction introduced in FHR decreases data dimensionality by a factor of up to 100 and reduces spectral noise.
elements.sent-2411-076: In our experiments, this resulted in a decrease in overall computational time by up to a factor of 10 and an increase in reconstruction SNR by up to 16dB.
elements.sent-2411-077: Bouman was partially supported by the Showalter Trust.
elements.sent-2411-078: This research used resources at the Spallation Neutron Source, a DOE Office of Science User Facility operated by the Oak Ridge National Laboratory.
elements.sent-2411-079: The beam time was allocated to the Spallation Neutrons and Pressure Diffractometer (SNAP) instrument on proposal number IPTS-25265.
elements.sent-2411-080: Hyperspectral neutron computed tomography (HSnCT) is a more advanced technique in which a pulsed neutron source illuminates a sample, and a time-of-flight detector measures the projection images across a range of wavelengths - potentially of the order of a few thousand.
elements.sent-2411-081: HSnCT reconstruction is challenging since data from potentially thousands of neutron wavelength bins must be reconstructed into individual 3D volumes.
elements.sent-2411-082: In DHR, data from each wavelength bin is reconstructed separately, typically using filtered back projection (FBP) due to its computational speed.
elements.sent-2411-083: However, even with FBP, DHR is still highly time-consuming since it may require thousands of 3D tomographic reconstructions.
elements.sent-2411-084: Moreover, producing high-quality reconstructions with FBP requires a large number of projections (also referred to as views) with high signal-to-noise ratios (SNR), which are extremely difficult to acquire due to time and resource constraints.
elements.sent-2411-085: Consequently, DHR reconstructions using FBP often suffer from severe noise and reconstruction artifacts.
elements.sent-2411-086: While DHR can alternatively use an advanced algorithm like model-based iterative reconstruction (MBIR) \cite{bouman2022mbir to improve quality, the computation time would be too high to make it practically implementable.}
elements.sent-2411-087: Substantially improves SNR in the reconstructions by approximating the data within a low-dimensional subspace.
elements.sent-2411-088: Significantly accelerates computation by reducing the required number of tomographic reconstructions.
elements.sent-2411-089: We apply our algorithm to measured neutron data collected at the Oak Ridge National Laboratory Spallation Neutron Source SNAP beamline and demonstrate that it is substantially faster and yields better results compared to the baseline DHR method.
elements.figure-2411-009: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figs/FHR_pipeline.png}} \caption{Illustration of the FHR algorithm. FHR first transforms the $N_k=1200$ dimensional hyperspectral views into $N_s=9$ dimensional subspace views. Then, it performs tomographic reconstruction to produce 9 subspace reconstructions. Finally, it expands the 9 subspace reconstructions into 1200 hyperspectral reconstructions.} \label{fig:FHR_pipeline}
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{figs/subspace_extraction.png} \caption{Subspace extraction: FHR employs NMF to decompose the $N_k=1200$ dimensional hyperspectral views ($p$) into $N_s=9$ dimensional subspace views ($V^s$) and corresponding subspace basis vectors ($D^s$). This approach effectively reduces data dimensions and also eliminates significant spectral noise.} \label{fig:sub_ext}
elements.sent-2411-090: Figure~\ref{fig:sub_ext illustrates the subspace extraction step of FHR in which the high-dimensional hyperspectral views $p \in \mathbb{R ^{N_p \times N_k}$ are decomposed into low-dimensional subspace views $V^s \in \mathbb{R ^{N_p \times N_s}$ and corresponding subspace basis vectors $D^s \in \mathbb{R ^{N_k \times N_s}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: The subspace dimension $N_s$ is chosen such that $N_m<N_s<<N_k$, where $N_m$ is the number of materials present in the sample.
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics[width=8cm]{figs/reconstruction.png} \caption{Tomographic reconstruction: FHR computes $N_s=9$ reconstructions from the extracted 9 sets of subspace views using MBIR.} \label{fig:recon}
elements.sent-2411-092: In order to perform MBIR, FHR uses the recently released SVMBIR Python package \cite{svmbir-code .}
elements.figure-2411-012: \begin{minipage}[a]{0.5\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figs/hyper_recons_real_DHR_FBP.png} \centerline{(a) DHR-FBP} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}[a]{0.5\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=0.85\linewidth]{figs/hyper_recons_real_DHR_MBIR.png} \centerline{(b) DHR-MBIR} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}[a]{\linewidth} \includegraphics[width=0.425\linewidth]{figs/hyper_recons_real_FHR.png} \centerline{(c) FHR} \end{minipage} \caption{Hyperspectral reconstruction of a moon-rock sample: (a) using the baseline DHR method with FBP, (b) using the baseline DHR method with MBIR, and (c) using the proposed FHR algorithm. The images shown are three different slices from the 3D volumes at three different wavelength bins. While DHR-MBIR reconstructions exhibit significantly fewer artifacts compared to DHR-FBP, they are still impacted by noise due to the low SNR per wavelength bin. In contrast, the proposed FHR dramatically improves the reconstruction quality by suppressing noise while preserving edge details.} \label{fig:recon_hyper_real}
elements.sent-2411-093: Below, we present results for hyperspectral reconstruction of a Moon rock sample from Apollo Mission 14, measured at the Spallation Neutron Source at Oak Ridge National Laboratory.
elements.sent-2411-094: HSnCT data was collected with $N_v=53$ views, $N_r=N_c=512$, and $N_k=1200$ hyperspectral wavelength bins.
elements.sent-2411-095: So, the resulting 3D volumes have dimensions $512\times 512\times 512$.
elements.sent-2411-096: We heuristically selected $N_s=9$ as the dimension of the subspace.
elements.sent-2411-097: Figure~\ref{fig:recon_hyper_real presents hyperspectral reconstructions for the measured data using DHR-FBP, DHR-MBIR, and the proposed FHR algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-098: Notice that while DHR-MBIR reconstructions demonstrate superior quality compared to DHR-FBP, they still contain significantly more noise and artifacts than FHR reconstructions.
elements.sent-2411-099: This is because of the initial subspace extraction in FHR, which removed the spectral noise orthogonal to the $N_s=9$ dimensional space.
elements.sent-2411-100: Table~\ref{table:hr_real shows a quantitative performance comparison among DHR-FBP, DHR-MBIR, and FHR.}
elements.sent-2411-101: For this dataset, FHR resulted in an SNR approximately 32 dB higher than DHR-FBP and 8.5 dB higher than DHR-MBIR.
elements.sent-2411-102: Moreover, FHR was over 10 times faster than DHR-FBP and over 100 times faster than DHR-MBIR, highlighting the computational efficiency of our approach.
elements.figure-2411-013: \begin{center} \caption{Quantitative performance comparison among DHR-FBP, DHR-MBIR, and the proposed FHR algorithm.} \label{table:hr_real} \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|} Algorithm SNR Computation Time DHR-FBP -6.18 dB 557.03 min DHR-MBIR 17.40 dB 6181.84 min FHR 25.94 dB 50.87 min \end{tabular} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-103: We present a fast hyperspectral reconstruction (FHR) algorithm for HSnCT that produces higher-quality reconstructions with dramatically less computation when compared to a conventional direct HSnCT reconstruction method.
elements.sent-2411-104: In our experiments, this resulted in a decrease in overall computational time by factors of 10 and 100 compared to DHR-FBP and DHR-MBIR, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-105: We also observed an increase in reconstruction SNR by approximately 32 dB over DHR-FBP and 8.5 dB over DHR-MBIR.
